coredata.dc:title: Investigating fluorescence quenching of cysteine-functionalized carbon quantum dots by heavy metal ions: Experimental and QSPR studies
coredata.dc:creator: Salahinejad, Maryam
Sadjadi, Sodeh
Abdouss, Majid
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have potential application as fluorescent probesfor the heavy metal detection. But there are few reports on the investigation of the quenching mechanism of CQDs by heavy metal ions. In this study, CQDs were prepared by directly pyrolyzing turmeric powder and functionalized using the thiol functionality of cysteine. Then the fluorescence quenching of cysteine-functionalized CQDs (Cys-CQDs) by 25 heavy metal ions were experimentally measured. Then the effect of the physical and chemical properties of heavy metal ions on the fluorescence quenching of CQDs was investigated by using the quantitative structure–property relationship (QSPR) method. Applying different machine learning method including genetic algorithm, enhanced replacement method, support vector machine, and multi linear regression, some reliable and predictive models were developed. Based on the variables included in the final QSPR model, covalent interactions of metal ions with functional groups on CQDs surface and the atomic environment were significant effect on the quenching of CQDs by heavy metal ions. Briefly, the tendency of metal ion to accept electrons from a donor ligand and the hard and soft acids and bases affinities can be considered as a primary factor governing the mechanism of quenching of CQDs by heavy metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cysteine-functionalized carbon quantum dots
Quantitative structure–property relationship
Fluorescence quenching
Heavy metal ions
Machine learning methods

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs derived from turmeric powder
Synthesis of cysteine-functionalized carbon quantum dots
Characterization of cysteine-functionalized carbon quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thiol-ene conjugation of L-cysteine to the CQDs.
TEM image (a) and DLS (b) results of cysteine-functionalized carbon quantum dots.
FT-IR spectrum of cysteine-functionalized carbon quantum dots.
Scatter plot of predicted versus experimental (I0/IQ) of Cys-CQD by heavy metal ions obtained by MLR and SVM modeling.
Selected descriptors for the sensitivity of fluorescence quenching (I0/IQ) of the Cys-CQD by heavy metal ions.
The Biplot of the first two PCs of PCA analysis.
Summary of statistical results and validation performance of MLR and SVM models.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Q2Loo: Leave one-out cross validation correlation coefficient.
Q2LGOn=4: Leave four-out cross validation correlation coefficient.

head.ce:title: Investigating fluorescence quenching of cysteine-functionalized carbon quantum dots by heavy metal ions: Experimental and QSPR studies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Radiation Application Research School, Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Radiation Application Research School, Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology, No. 350, Hafez Ave., Valiasr Square, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology, No. 350, Hafez Ave., Valiasr Square, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have potential application as fluorescent probesfor the heavy metal detection. But there are few reports on the investigation of the quenching mechanism of CQDs by heavy metal ions. In this study, CQDs were prepared by directly pyrolyzing turmeric powder and functionalized using the thiol functionality of cysteine. Then the fluorescence quenching of cysteine-functionalized CQDs (Cys-CQDs) by 25 heavy metal ions were experimentally measured. Then the effect of the physical and chemical properties of heavy metal ions on the fluorescence quenching of CQDs was investigated by using the quantitative structure–property relationship (QSPR) method. Applying different machine learning method including genetic algorithm, enhanced replacement method, support vector machine, and multi linear regression, some reliable and predictive models were developed. Based on the variables included in the final QSPR model, covalent interactions of metal ions with functional groups on CQDs surface and the atomic environment were significant effect on the quenching of CQDs by heavy metal ions. Briefly, the tendency of metal ion to accept electrons from a donor ligand and the hard and soft acids and bases affinities can be considered as a primary factor governing the mechanism of quenching of CQDs by heavy metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals can be easily released by various industries and natural resources processing into the three paramount ecological sources namely air, soil, and water. In view of the high toxicity and deleterious effects of heavy metals on humans and the environment, there have been an ascending demand and public concern about monitoring and eliminating them from ecological resources in the world. Their non-biodegradability, bioaccumulation, environmental persistence, and trace-level toxicity make it important to track their levels and fate over time [1]. Consequently, many endeavors have been made to establish reliable, fast, and cost-effective methods for the qualitative and quantitative assessment of heavy metals from biological and environmental samples.

Generally, conventional techniques such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS)[2,3], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission/mass spectrometry (ICP-AES/ICP-MS) [4], X-ray fluorescence analysis [5], chromatography [6,7], and electrochemical methods [8] have been utilized for the qualitative and quantitative evaluation of metals in various biological and environmental samples. However, some drawbacks of these methods, including off-site sampling and complicated sample preparation, high operational cost, expensive instrumentation, and difficulty in real-time assessments, have encouraged researchers to find alternative methods.

In recent years, highly sensitive and selective techniques have been developed to quantitatively and qualitatively detect various heavy metal ions [8–10]. In the meantime, fluorescent sensors have attracted interest in heavy metal ion analysis because of certain analytical merits, such as ultra-high sensitivity and selectivity, simplicity, fast response time, as well as on-site sampling, and real-time measurement [11]. The fluorescent probes are the key factor in the performance of fluorescent sensors [11]. A broad variety of materials, including metallic and organic nanoparticles [12,13], organic dyes compound [14], mesoporous materials [15], metal–organic frameworks [16], and fluorescent aptamers [17] have been identified as suitable probes and used for heavy metal detection. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as one of the most groundbreaking carbon-based nanomaterials at the forefront of fluorescent probes, have greatly helped to extend the option of sensing media to heavy metal detection [18,19]. CQDs owe their pioneering applications due to their outstanding properties including low toxicity, high specific surface areas, high dispersibility in different solvents, good chemical inertness, functionalization, and modification capacity [11]. Fascinating features such as low cost, accessibility, and eco-friendly as well as having diverse functional group on the surface of natural carbon resources, ranging from fruit juices and spices to organic wastes, make them as significant precursors to the production of natural CQDs (N-CQDs) [18].

An opportune understanding of the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CQDs can be very influential in the design and development of novel fluorescent probes in a systematic and expeditious investigation [19]. While the applications of different CQDs-based fluorescent probes in chemical and biological analysis have intensified, less attention has been paid to fundamental research investigating the quenching mechanism of CQDs due to the structural complexity of CQDs and their derivatives.

The quantitative structure–activity/property relationship (QSAR/QSPR) methods establish the mathematical equations based on the correlation of physicochemical and structural features of compounds to their activity/property [20]. Although QSPR approaches are known as multipurpose methods in the modeling and prediction of activities or properties of various organic and inorganic compounds, they are also used to investigate the mechanism of action between inhibitors and enzymes [21,22]. To the best of our knowledge, there is no QSPR study about the interaction mechanism between CQDs and heavy metals. In view of this, N-CQDs were prepared by directly pyrolyzing turmeric powder and functionalized using the thiol functionality of cysteine, and then the effect of the physical and chemical properties of heavy metal ions on the fluorescence quenching of CQDs was investigated by using the QSPR method. The fluorescence quenching of cysteine-functionalized CQDs (Cys-CQDs) by 25 heavy metal ions was experimentally measured. The most important parameters involved in the established QSPR model were then used in order to explore the interactions between Cys-CQDs and different heavy metal ions and to consider the fluorescence quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs derived from turmeric powder
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were prepared by directly pyrolyzing turmeric powder. In a typical procedure, 2 g turmeric powder was directly heated at 250 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, the obtained dark black product was cooled to room temperature and mechanically ground to fine powders. The product was then diluted by addition of deionized water and sonicated for 15 min. The diluted dispersion was then filtered through a 0.2 μm microfiltration membrane to separate large particles and then stored at 4 °C for further characterization and use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cysteine-functionalized carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows a schematic illustration of the synthesis of cysteine-functionalized CQDs. Aiming to enhance metal-chelating property, the prepared CQDs were functionalized using the thiol functionality of cysteine through thiol-ene click chemistry. The synthetic procedure consists of two-steps. First, the carbonyl functionalities of CQDs were reduced by using sodium borohydride. In a typical experiment, 10 ml of CQDs solution was treated with a large excess of NaBH4 (0.5 g) under stirring at room temperature for 40 min. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was dialyzed using 500 Da cut-off dialysis bag against water for 2 × 12 h to remove reductive agent, obtaining the reduced CQDs. Second, the covalent surface functionalization of reduced CQDs with L-cysteine was carried out by addition of ammonium persulfate (2 g) to 20 ml solution of the reduced CQDs, which introduce ene-terminated surface. The reaction was allowed to proceed for 1 h at 90 °C. Then, 0.05 g L-cysteine was added and reaction mixture was heated for an additional 24 h. Finally, the obtained solution of thiol-linked L-Cys-CQDs was obtained by microfiltration (through 0.2 μm microfiltration membrane) followed by dialyzing in a dialysis bag (cut off molecular weight 500 Da) against water for 2 × 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of cysteine-functionalized carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The size distribution and morphology of the synthesized cysteine-functionalized carbon quantum dots were considered by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) characterization. These data were obtained with a Zeiss EM10C transmission electron microscope (Germany) and Malvern particle size analyzer (UK) respectively. The TEM image of the synthesized L-Cys-CQDs and their size distribution in Fig. 2 indicate a fairly uniform size distribution of sample with an average diameter of about 7 nm and spherical shape.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrum (FT-IR) of the sample was recorded by Brucker Vector 22 spectrometer (Germany) in the spectral range of 4000–400 cm−1 (Fig. 3 ). The vibrational peaks of Cys-CQDs appear at 3467–3305 and 3215, 1637, 1200, and 1107 cm−1 for stretching vibration band of N-H, –OH, C = O, asymmetric C-O-C stretching, and symmetric C-O-C stretching, respectively. The spectrum also presents a band at 694 cm−1 corresponding to C-S stretching vibration and no absorption peak for thiol group (S-H) is observable in the range of 2670–2550 cm−1, confirming the cysteine immobilization through the thiol functionality.

Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed with a Perkin Elmer LS 55 photo Luminescence Spectrometer (USA) with photomultiplier array detector and pulsed Xenon flash lamp at line frequency 50 Hz over 200–1100 nm spectral range with spectral accuracy of 1 nm. All the fluorescence measurements were performed in a phosphate buffer (0.1 mol/l, pH 7.0) at room temperature. Typically, 400 µl of the synthesized L-Cys-CQDs was added to 1.0 ml of buffer solution and mixed for 10 s. Subsequently, 10 μl of standard solution of different heavy metal ion was added to the above solution and mixed thoroughly. Finally, the fluorescence spectra recorded against a blank solution containing the same ingredient except for the metal ions. Maximum photoluminescence emission wavelength of Cys-CQDs peaked at 310 nm.

The fluorescence behavior of Cys-CQDs was examined in the presence of various heavy metal ions comprised of Aluminum (Al3+), Arsenic (As5+), Gold (Au1+), Barium (Ba2+), Bismuth (Bi3+), Calcium (Ca2+), Cadmium (Cd2+), Cobalt (Co2+), Chromium (Cr2+), Copper (Cu2+), Iron (Fe2+), Mercury (Hg2+), Lithium (Li+), Magnesium (Mg2+), Manganese (Mn2+), Nickel (Ni2+), Lead (Pb+2), Palladium (Pd2+), Selenium (Se2-), Tin (Sn2+), Titanium (Ti2+), Vanadium (V2+), Tungsten (W4+), Zinc (Zn2+) and Zirconium (Zr4+).
The data set for the construction of QSPR models comprises all 25 heavy metal ions experimentally examined for fluorescence quenching.

Different physical and chemical properties of metal ions e.g. atomic weight (AW), periodic number and atomic number (AN), electron affinity and electronegativity (X), metallic and ionic radius (r), ionization energies, charge, valence electron, atomic volume, melting and boiling points, density, and so on were used as independent variables or descriptors. These properties of metal ions extracted from the database on properties of chemical elements [23], RSC periodic table [24], which are freely available online, and handbook of chemistry and physics [25,26].

Some new descriptorshave beenadditionallydefinedviacombination and mathematical transformation of somesignificantdescriptors.For example, since the ionic radius is so important in metal ion interactions, mathematical transformations of the ionic radius (ri), such as the inverse of ionic radius (1/ri), the ionic radius square (ri 2), and the inverse ionic radius square (1/ri 2), have been defined as new descriptors. Combining ionic radius with another significant element, such as ionic charge (Z), yielded new descriptors including charge to ionic radius (z/ri) and charge to inverse ionic radius square (z/ri 2). Finally, to construct QSPR models, more than 100 descriptors have been generated and used as independent variables.

The sensitivity of fluorescence quenching of Cys-CQD by heavy metal ions, which was calculated as the ratio of the areas of photoluminescence peak of Cys-CQD solutions before (I0) and after (IQ) metal ion addition, was considered a dependent variable (I0/IQ) in QSPR modeling.

Stepwise regression, genetic algorithm (GA) and enhanced replacement method (ERM) techniques were used to extract the more informative variables from the descriptor pool. In QSPR analysis, the stepwise regression technique is commonly used for selecting an acceptable and restricted number of descriptors. It is one of the simplest and most appropriate methods for the choice of descriptors in QSAR studies [27]. However, stepwise regression does not provide satisfactory results when the number of descriptors is high.

In GA method, a search technique focusing on natural evolution and selection, a subset of variables was selected based on a fitness criterion via the process of random mutation, cross-over, recombination [28,29]. The GA approach has been widely applied in molecular modeling and QSPR/QSAR studies for variable selection, data reduction and optimization [30].

The ERM method, a full search algorithm proposed by Mercader et al. [31], uses linear regression procedure to generate models with lowest standard deviation (S) and specify the optimal number of descriptors based on the descriptor pool search. Since modeling errors in the replacement of variables are taken into account, the ERM approach is expected to show better statistical results than GA approaches in which the descriptors are substituted as random. You will find more comprehensive details about these algorithms in the references [31,32].

The multiple linear regression (MLR) method, which applies extensively in linear problems, models the relationship between two or more independent or predictor variables and a dependent or criterion answer variable by fitting a linear mathematical equation to experimental data. A useful supervised machine learning algorithm for solving pattern recognition, classification, and regression problems is the support vector machine (SVM), introduced by Vapnik [33]. A SVM constructs and uses a hyperplane or collection of hyperplans to find the perfect hyperplane for properly classifying data points used for classification, regression, or other tasks. SVM is increasingly popular and has obtained broad applications in various aspects due to its benefits and remarkable generalization efficiency over other techniques [34,35].

Principal component analysis (PCA) has been extensively applied as a useful pattern recognition tool to track the similarities and differences between different samples. The PCA method is based on dimension reduction in a set of variables by constructing a number of uncorrelated variables as principal components (PC). The correlation coefficients between the original variables and a new principal component indicate the 'weight' of each single variable in the component and are called component loadings [36]. PCAplots are typical two-dimensional display method of PCA analysis, were used to explore the differences or similarities between samples and the patterns in space defined by independent variables used to construct model.

Each QSPR model is evaluated using statistical indices such as the squared conventional correlation coefficient (R2 c), correlation coefficient of prediction (R2 p), standard error of calibration (SEC), and standard error of prediction (SEP). The leave-one-out (LOO-CV) and leave-group-out (LGO) cross validation procedure are also used to assess the internal prediction power and efficiency of the resulting models. In the LOO-CV process, samples were applied to create a QSPR model from a total data set (n-1) and then the property of the left-out sample was evaluated by the constructed model. This procedure was repeated until all the samples were removed once in the entire data set. In the case of LGO-CV, a group of the data (n-k) was excluded from the data set and the model was then refitted; the predicted values were then compared to the actual values for the omitted data [37]. The robustness of the model is tested by a y-randomization test in which the dependent variable vector, (I0/IQ), has been randomly shuffled and a new QSPR model has been designed using the original independent variable matrix [38]. The low value of randomization correlation determination (R2 Yrand) values indicate that the models obtained for the given data set are appropriate models and do not display any correlation of chance [38]. All calculations were performed by using the MATLAB (version 8.6, Math Works, Inc., Natick, MA, USA).

Heavy metals can effectively influence the fluorescence intensity by significantly changing the rate of intersystem crossing through external heavy atom effect. Via the transition from triplet to singlet states or vice versa, the complexation of heavy metal ions with fluorescence indicators results in either increased or decreased fluorescence. Fluorescence quenching, defined as any physicochemical process that decreases the fluorescence intensity, typically follows simple Stern-Volmer kinetics in a solution by the external heavy atom effect [39]. The sensitivity of fluorescence quenching can be effectively related to the quenching rate (Kq) based on the Stern-Volmer equation, as shown below Eq. (1):
(1) I0/IQ = 1 + Kq.τo[Q]
where I0 and IQ are the fluorescent intensities of fluorescence indicators in the absence and presence of heavy metal, respectively, τo is the fluorescence lifetime in the absence of the quencher, and [Q] is known as the quencher or heavy metal concentration.

Among the various variable selection methods, excellent and significantly better models were provided with the selected descriptors extracted by the ERM method than those extracted by stepwise and GA methods. This can be explained by the fact that GA is a global search technique that relies on the initial variable set, while the ERM is a total search algorithm that shows less tendency to stay in local minima [31,40].

In order to relate the sensitivity of fluorescence quenching of Cys-CQD by heavy metal ions (I0/IQ) as the dependent variable to the selected heavy metal ion descriptors, the MLR and SVM methods were applied. The statistical results and validation performance of MLR and SVM models obtained on the basis of R2 c, Q2 (LOO), Q2 LGOn=4, R2 Yrand, R2 p, and SEC and SEP error values were listed in Table 1 . Fig. 4 displays the scatter plot of predicted versus experimental (I0/IQ) of Cys-CQD by heavy metal ions obtained by MLR and SVM modeling. The resulting data shows that the SVM model is excellent and significantly better than the MLR model. SVM can handle both linear and nonlinear regression, so it can fit complex nonlinear relationships between dependent and independent variables. As a result, it generates better models than linear methods such as the MLR method. However, the MLR model provides a simple linear QSPR equation with an interpretable mathematical expression.

Covalent index (CovIn), atomic environment number (AEN), and valance electron number (NVE) were the best subset of descriptors chosen by the ERM variable selection technique. A three-variable equation was given by the MLR model based on the selected descriptors and the (I0/IQ) of Cys-CQD by heavy metal ions (eq. 2):
(2) (I0/IQ) = (2.643 ± 0.231)CovIn + (0.275 ± 0.051)AEN − (0.235 ± 0.027)NVE − (5.115 ± 0.863)

The variables included in the final QSPR model can reveal the important parameters for investigating fluorescence quenching of Cys-CQD by heavy metal ions and can help to understand the possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching of Cys-CQDs by heavy metal ions. Fig. 5 displays the selected descriptors for the sensitivity of fluorescence quenching (I0/IQ) of the Cys-CQD by heavy metal ions. As can be inferred from equation (2) and Fig. 5, covalent index (CovIn) was the most significant descriptor. The covalent index (X2 mr) [41], in which Xm is metal ion electronegativity and r is ionic radius of metal, describes the importance of covalent interactions relative to ionic interactions. The covalent index is a representation of the metal ion's tendency to accept electrons from a donor ligand, so the higher the value of the covalent index of a metal ion, the greater its ability to form covalent bonds with Cys-CQD. Metal ions with high covalent index, such as Sn2+, Se2-, and Mn2+, which have stronger propensity to form stable complex with Cys-CQD, cause in higher fluorescence quenching of Cys-CQD and thus low (I0/IQ) values.

The second important variable involved in MLR and SVM models is the atomic environment number (AEN). AEN is referred to as the polyhedron formed by surrounding atoms up to the distance of the maximum gap in the radial distribution function [42]. Therefore, AEN defines the number of neighbors and interatomic distances that define a coordination polyhedron around the central atom, the spatial structure of the ligand atoms closely attached to the central atom or ion [43]. Atomic environment contains useful information about involving number of neighboring atom, bond angles, and local electronic states, that can affect binding energies characteristic of carbon nanomaterials [44]. The valance electrons are responsible for the interaction between atoms and the forming of chemical bonds, and the valance electron number (NVE) is thenumberofelectrons that an atom must obtain or lose to achieve the electronicconfiguration of the closest noble gas. The hard and soft acids and bases (HSAB) theory is specifically related to valence electrons. The thiol-ene conjugation of L-cysteine to the CQDs synthesized was confirmed through experimental studies in previous section (Fig. 3), so the functional groups responsible for interaction with heavy metal ions are NH2 and COOH groups. The hard bases of NH2 and COOH groups on the Cys-CQD prefer hard acids such as Cr3+, Mg2+ and Zr4+ resulting in higher fluorescence quenching of Cys-CQD.

There is a great deal of data in a biplot of PCA analysis which can be helpful in understanding relationships between variables and samples. Fig. 6 displays the biplot of the first two PCs of PCA analysis (explaining 56.52% and 35.06 of the variance respectively). The PCA biplot depicts PC scores of samples (metal ions) and loadings of variables (selected descriptors). As can be seen from this figure, metal ions with similar quenching behavior are grouped together. Main group metals such as Bi, As, Sn, and Se tend to behave differently from transition elements such as Cd, Co, Ni and Zinc. There are also similar patterns of doubly charged metal ions, and most of them are found near each other. The large angles between CovIN, AEN and VEN variables indicated no correlation between these variables.

In this research, fluorescence measurements of the synthesized cysteine-functionalized carbon quantum dots by 25 heavy metal ions were investigated. In order to correlate the sensitivity of fluorescence quenching of Cys-CQD by heavy metal ions (I0/IQ) to the properties of heavy metal ions, some QSPR models were developed using various machine learning methods.

The selected metal properties, extracted by ERM variable selection and involved in the final QSPR model, implicated the importance of covalent interactions of metal ions with functional group on CQDs surface, the atomic environment involving number of neighboring atom, bond angles, and local electronic states were significant effect governing the mechanism quenching of CQDs by heavy metal ions. Both MLR and SVM models satisfactory statistical performance.

The variables included in the final QSPR model can reveal the important parameters for investigating fluorescence quenching of Cys-CQD by heavy metal ions and can help to understand the possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching of Cys-CQDs by heavy metal ions. In short, the effect of the tendency of metal ion to accept electrons from a donor ligand and the HSAB affinities may be considered as primary factor in the quenching mechanism of CQDS by metal ions. The CQD fluorescent probe for hard metal ions, such as Li+ and Al3+, is therefore proposed to be functionalized by ligands containing relatively nonpolarizable donor atoms (e.g. N, F and O) and to be functionalized by ligands containing relatively polarizable donor atoms (e.g. S, P, and Cl) for soft metal ions such as Ag+ and Hg+2.
Maryam Salahinejad: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Sodeh Sadjadi: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Majid Abdouss: Resources, Review.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
